mappings.rationaleAI: Human skeletal system anatomy and head/neck structures directly address the human skeletal system (B.7), human integument (A.7), vertebrate skeleton structure (B.4), and vertebrate muscle systems (C.5).
Motor responses and physiological regulation across organ systems address the human integument (A.7), human skeletal system (B.7), and vertebrate muscle function and regulation (C.5.c).
Morphological features across phyla including Porifera spicules and Echinodermata ossicles address invertebrate integuments (A.2), invertebrate skeleton (B.3), and invertebrate muscle systems (C.4).
Morphogenesis and Hox gene patterning during embryonic development address the embryology of vertebrate skeletons (B.4.b).
Dental anatomy including enamel and dentine as cuticular structures (B.2.a), evolutionary diversity of tooth forms across vertebrates (B.4), and properties of bone-like dental tissues (B.6).

Mammalian morphological diversity including hair as a skin derivative (A.3.b), skin variations among vertebrate classes (A.4), vertebrate skeletal adaptations for locomotion (B.4), and vertebrate muscle systems (C.5).
Reptilian integument with epidermal scales and physiological adaptations for terrestrial life (A.3.b, A.4), and skeletal innovations enabling land colonization (B.4).
Insect exoskeletons as invertebrate integuments (A.2), cuticular structures forming external skeletons (B.3), and insect muscle systems (C.4).
Ornamental traits including feathers, horns, and antlers used in mate competition are skin derivatives and appendages (A.3.b).
Evolutionary framework explaining adaptation relates to embryology and evolution of vertebrate skin (A.5) and skeletal evolution (B.4.b).

Covers cell structure, differentiation, and diversity including prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, relevant to general features of body covering, simple contractile systems, and complex contractile tissues.
Covers hormonal regulation including growth hormone affecting muscular and skeletal function, relevant to function and regulation of muscle action.
